content.full_text:
April 28, 1977


Robert M. Sweet, Esq.
State Bar of California
1230 West Third Street
Los Angeles, CA 90017

Dear Bob:

You recently called me to inquire whether a public official
who is an attorney has to disclose the names of his clients under
Government Code Section 87207(b) in the following two situations:

1. The attorney's law firm is located within his official
jurisdiction, within the meaning of Section 82030 of
the Act1/, but his client is located outside the juris-
diction.

2. The attorney's law firm is located outside the juris-
diction and the client is located within the jurisdiction.

In the first example, the law firm would be reportable if
it is a source of income of $250 or more, but the client would not
be. In the second example, the law firm would not be reportable, but
the client would have to be disclosed provided that the attorney held
a 10% or greater interest in his firm (Section 82030(a)) and the at-
torney's pro rata share of the client's fee was $1,000 or more
(Section 87207(b)).

The reason for this conclusion is that income may be de-
rived from more than one source. In the case of a law firm, a source
of income may be not only the firm, but also the client. Although
Section 87207(b) of the Act suggests that clients need be disclosed
only if income from the law firm itself must be disclosed, the con-
trolling language is found in Section 82030, which provides that
"income" means "income of any nature from any source" and excludes
only those "sources" located outside the official's jurisdiction.

_____________
1/ Section 82030 provides that a source of income is in-
side the official's jurisdiction, and therefore reportable, unless
the source of income is located outside the jurisdiction, or is "not
doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do business
within the jurisdiction, or not having done business within the
jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any statement
or other action is required under this title."

2,229

Robert M. Sweet, Esq.
Page two
April 28, 1977

Accordingly, the client, as an independent source of income, may
be disclosable even if the official's law firm is not.

I trust the foregoing answers your questions. If I can
be of any further assistance to you, please let me know.

Sincerely,

Michael J. Baker
Litigation Counsel

MJB:jo

sections.question: Whether a public official who is an attorney has to disclose the names of his clients under Government Code Section 97207 in the following two situations: 1. The attorney's law firm is located within his official jurisdiction, but his client is located outside the jurisdiction. 2. The attorney's law firm is located outside the jurisdiction and the client is located within the jurisdiction.
sections.conclusion: In the first example, the law firm would be reportable if it is a source of income of $250 or more, but the client would not be. In the second example, the law firm would not be reportable, but the client would have to be disclosed provided that the attorney held a 50% or greater interest in his firm and the attorney's pro rata share of the client's fee was $1,000 or more. The client, as an independent source of income, may be discloseable even if the official's law firm is not.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a public official attorney disclose client names under Government Code Section 97207 when the law firm and client are located in different jurisdictions relative to the official's jurisdiction?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The reportability of law firm income and client names depends on whether they are sources of income located within the official's jurisdiction. Clients may be discloseable as independent income sources even when the law firm itself is not reportable, if the attorney has a 50%+ interest and receives $1,000+ in pro rata fees.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with OCR errors and unclear formatting. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' headers but Q/C structure is evident. Dates appear to be April 23 and April 26, 1977. Addressee is Witt H. Sweet, Attorney for State of California. Signed by Michael J. Baker, Litigation Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a public official who is an attorney has to disclose the names of his clients under Government Code Section 97207 in the following two situations: 1. The attorney's law firm is located within his official jurisdiction, but his client is located outside the jurisdiction. 2. The attorney's law firm is located outside the jurisdiction and the client is located within the jurisdiction.

CONCLUSION: In the first example, the law firm would be reportable if it is a source of income of $250 or more, but the client would not be. In the second example, the law firm would not be reportable, but the client would have to be disclosed provided that the attorney held a 50% or greater interest in his firm and the attorney's pro rata share of the client's fee was $1,000 or more. The client, as an independent source of income, may be discloseable even if the official's law firm is not.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that public official attorneys must disclose client names as separate income sources under Government Code Section 97207 when clients are located within their official jurisdiction, regardless of where the law firm is located, applying the principle that income may be derived from multiple sources.
